The IRC lib is opinionated about the expected character encoding, requiring UTF-8 from all clients (which is not a requirement in the specification). The IRC library provides several techniques for overriding the encoding, which are detailed in the Decoding Input section of [the homepage](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/irc) for the library.

The reason different clients have different behavior probably has to do with the way that each of those clients handle errors, as the IRC library itself is consistent in its error handling.

Traditionally, renegotiation is something that can be legally triggered at any time by the remote peer; while opinion seems to have shifted against it these days, the original idea was that libraries should automatically and transparently trigger it on some kind of timer. (One of the major motivations for renegotiation is to put an upper bound on how much data get transferred under a single key, so for long-lived connections you're supposed to do it every so often.) Just not supporting renegotiation is a option, but it should be clear that it's not spec compliant (except for when speaking HTTP/2) and may cause interoperability problems.

Hi! there seems to be several different questions here.

The way to check if a variables is an instance of Quantities, the way to do it is:

```python
>>> ureg = pint.UnitRegistry()
>>> isinstance(x, ureg.Quantity)
```

Regarding heterogenous list of Quantitities, that is not a problem. What you cannot do is a heterogenous numpy array quantity. i.e. numpy arrays can be the magnitude of a quantity and therefore all elements have the same units

Regarding creating code that can handle values with and without units, you can use [wraps](http://pint.readthedocs.io/en/latest/wrapping.html) with `strict=False`

Finally, if you want to use pint in different projects we are trying to create a better solution for that (see #623)

OK. I've run the bench tests. I assume you're only interested in the `bench_numpy.yaml` output, so that's the only piece I'm including. The results for `bench_base.yaml` are--obviously--all basically the same.

Using NumPy 10.4 (pre `__numpy_ufunc__` functionality), the results of the bench tests are basically the same regardless of whether I use [**pint/master**](https://github.com/lkilcher/pint), or my [**lkilcher/dev**](https://github.com/lkilcher/pint/commit/b4e2b4b74e23925c06f0c69f67b2ee6db0ee2f20) branch. This makes sense because NumPy 10.4 is unaware of `__numpy_ufunc__` and so it simply uses the old `__array_wrap__` + `__array_prepare__` functionality in both cases.  The results for this case are:

pint master + NumPy 10.4
-------

> 5.77e-07   NumPy cosine (base)
5.85e+01   NumPy cosine radian (normalized)
7.09e+01   NumPy cosine dimensionless (normalized)
1.05e+02   NumPy cosine degree (normalized)

When I switch to NumPy-dev, the results change dramatically for both **pint master** *and* **lkilcher/dev**:

pint master + NumPy dev
-------
>5.82e-07   NumPy cosine (base)
6.77e+02   NumPy cosine radian (normalized)
6.99e+02   NumPy cosine dimensionless (normalized)
5.73e+02   NumPy cosine degree (normalized)

lkilcher/dev + NumPy dev
---------

> 5.73e-07   NumPy cosine (base)
4.65e+01   NumPy cosine radian (normalized)
5.55e+01   NumPy cosine dimensionless (normalized)
8.71e+01   NumPy cosine degree (normalized)

What's interesting here is that **pint master + NumPy dev** is an order of magnitude slower than **pint master + NumPy 10.4**. Perhaps thisthis is a result of my NumPy 10.4 having been optimized in some way? In that case, why is the `NumPy cosine (base)` test the same in all three cases? The other option is that something else in the NumPy core has changed.

Anyway, on the plus side, the **lkilcher/dev + NumPy dev** results look like they are an order of magnitude faster than **pint master + NumPy dev**, and about 20% faster than **pint master + NumPy 10.4**.

Actually @reverbc, you're right, consistency within the same version of faker is the most important. The tests for my plugin for tests just get this problem because they're on the meta level, I guess I should expect that.

It'd be slow since it's a race condition but the logic was basically this:

```python
import random
import faker

fake = faker.Faker()

for i in range(0, random.randint(1, 1000)):
    collection_start = fake.past_datetime()

    for j in range(0, random.randint(1, 100000)):
        # This was used as a fake file creation time:
        fake.past_datetime(start_date=collection_start)
```

Looking at this a bit more, my comment above wasn't quite right – `random.randint` handles the case where it's called with the same value – but the error is the same as what I get calling `fake.past_datetime(start_date='now')` so it must have been a timing race where the first call returned within a second of the current time and then when `past_datetime` did the `-1s` call it was effectively calling `randint(1, 0)` and getting that error. That'd fit with it happening very infrequently out of millions of calls. I'm not sure whether it'd be worth handling it inclusively and just returning the start_date when the end date is less than or equal to the value or simply documenting that you need to use `date_time_between` if you can't guarantee that `start_date` is at least a couple seconds in the past.
@kungfu71186 I think the issue is that the range of `randint` increments by 1 every second. Try putting a `time.sleep(1)` somewhere in the loop

Though some of these might reduce the numbers of lines of code, the main choice in what would be possible is not convenience rather than readability, since the main idea (as it strikes me) is to produce readable fitting, 

With that in mind I would say that the `Variables` object will have to have more input and not use introspection (?) to find the number of variables to be defined. The string added, like sympy, would be the better option, though the formatting bothers me. I would want to see (compulsory?):

    x, y = Variables('x, y') 

Note the added comma, which in PEP8 is the best way to represent multiple variables. It might be however, that some fuzziness should be allowed (PEP8 is a style *guide*) and I'm not sure on the implementation of that.

As for the Django-esque syntax, though it might be beautiful, I would at the very least not introduce it as standard. If it were possible for Django users and not be the norm, I would ~~not object~~ encourage it.

> But After I encrypt use the lib, I can't decrypt it with another C program. The C program only decrypt 16 bytes data.

Please read the readme https://github.com/ifduyue/xxtea#xxtea--

> The XXTEA algorithm takes a 128-bit key and operates on an array of 32-bit integers (at least 2 integers), but it doesn't define the conversions between bytes and array. Due to this reason, many XXTEA implementations out there are not compatible with each other.

You can only encrypt and decrypt using this python lib, as it's not compatible of any other libs.

Another workaround that can probably be used is to change the permissions on the system created evdev device after grabbing it in order to disable all read access for new processes. That way, no application launched after ds4drv will have access to that device. Running `chmod 000 <device>` is a workaround that comes in handy under various circumstances. You can always reset the file permissions if you want to undo the change and allow other applications to have access to the device again; that probably wouldn't be desired in this case though.

That's a good point - finding the sysfs of the real device from `/proc/bus/input/devices` and monitoring it to see when the path disappears seems to be a pretty reliable way to detect when the controller is disconnected. I can similarly monitor `/proc/bus/input/devices` to see when a controller is connected.

It doesn't quite solve the situation of multiple controllers, but that's an edge case I don't need to worry about right now.

I checked the picture. The conclusion is that picture's exif data is wrong.
"Scene Type(in exif ifd, tag number is 41729)" value should be UNDEFINED type. It is written in exif official document(http://www.cipa.jp/std/documents/e/DC-008-2012_E.pdf). But the picture's "Scene Type" value is saved as SHORT. 
This library's "load" function just reads binary to get exif. On the other hand, "dump" function converts each value with reference to appropritate type. If exif values are saved as wrong type, they should be corrected before using "dump" function.

It's a known issue, without a good work-around.
Currently, if I want to clear the cache, I run `sudo ~/.cache/bazel/`, but this is a terrible idea.

More to the point, originally, we've designed the cache to work in tandem with bazel, so that if I do `dazel build` and then right away `bazel build`, it'll be the same exact cache. Back when that feature worked, the workaround would be to run `bazel clean`. This feature broke long ago (I think somewhere around bazel 0.3.0 or even earlier).

The ticket then should be one of the following:
Either fix the cache to work with bazel (although, i'm not hopeful it'll hold for very long again, due to changes in bazel),
or redesign the cache to work completely separate from bazel, and thus make it way simpler.

According to the SecureTransport reference, SecEvaluateTrust can trigger network access. https://developer.apple.com/library/ios/documentation/Security/Reference/certifkeytrustservices/index.html#//apple_ref/c/func/SecTrustEvaluate

Apparently that is not for CRL/OCSP checking, but network-hosted trust roots. So manual validation using SecTrustEvaluateAsync would need to be used to prevent blocking on that.

> I must admit, I am not too familiar with other formats, SDF, MOL2, etc. Are these each handled through their own object, similar to PandasPdb, e.g., PandasSdf? 

SDF is probably the most popular format for small molecules I'd say. I work with small molecules a lot (actually much more often than with proteins and PDB files) but I almost exclusively work with MOL2 files. The reason is that SDF doesn't store partial charge information, which is (almost) crucial for analyzing electrostatic features and creating pharmacophores imho. But back to the question: there's currently a PandasMol2, analogous to PandasPdb, as well (http://rasbt.github.io/biopandas/tutorials/Working_with_MOL2_Structures_in_DataFrames/). I am going to make a PandasSdf at some point, but it isn't on my priority list, yet. 

> If that's the case, I think having a separate standard for each, aligned with the SDF, MOL2, etc, standards makes sense.

Yeah, so now that final's week is over, I am probably going to change the column format to the original specs this weekend or so -- rather sooner than later. I am currently using PandasMol2 in one of my research projects for some analysis and planning to submit the manuscript in 2-3 weeks coupled with a software package, so I'd rather like to make this change now before I have to worry about dependencies and versioning and those things.

> Another idea/approach that may make sense would be to have some base Molecule class that would have methods to populate properties by reading in different files, PDB, SDF, MOL2, etc (though what if the structure model is actually multiple molecules? maybe you would want a System class that inherits from Molecule?). In this situation, it would be best to either have a single standard way to query/return molecule properties, e.g., residue name, coordinates, etc, or to accommodate the standard for each file type by having multiple queries for the same thing.

I was thinking about that when I made the PandasMol2 class. However, I think that's generally not feasible because the formats are so different. I.e., the methods would have to be implemented for each class (PandasMol2, PandasPdb) individually anyway, so inheritance would be kind of overkill. The idea right now is to make the method names at least consistent. Also, with future features in mind, I'd rather prefer not to oppose any common denominator requirements at this point. Working with proteins vs small molecules (e.g., for ligand-based virtual screening) require very different workflows etc.

One future feature could be a converter function though, which converts one DataFrame-based class to another (`a_pandasMol2_instance = convert(in=a_PandasPdb_instance, out=PandasMol2`)

```
class LockableMPDClient(MPDClient):
    def __init__(self, use_unicode=False):
        super(LockableMPDClient, self).__init__()
        self.use_unicode = use_unicode
        self._lock = Lock()
    def acquire(self):
        self._lock.acquire()
    def release(self):
        self._lock.release()
    def __enter__(self):
        self.acquire()
    def __exit__(self, type, value, traceback):
        self.release()
```

Usage is:

```
MPD = LockableMPDClient() # for sending commands and getting status
with MPD:
     ..stuff using the locked MPD...
```

#!/bin/bash
apt-get install pkg-config build-essential autoconf automake python libstatgrab-dev python-all-dev

wget http://ftp.uk.i-scream.org/sites/ftp.i-scream.org/pub/i-scream/pystatgrab/pystatgrab-0.5.tar.gz
tar zxvf pystatgrab-0.5.tar.gz
cd pystatgrab-0.5/
./setup.py build
./setup.py install

cd /opt
wget https://github.com/downloads/nicolargo/glances/glances-1.1.3.tar.gz
tar xvfz glances-1.1.3.tar.gz
cd glances-1.1.3
./configure
make
make install


Merci

I think this specific error has to do with how sections are read rather than a regex error. 

It appears that lasio looks for lines that start with a `~` and adds any subsequent non-commented lines to the `sect_lines` list of the `read_file_contents` OrderedDict (see [line 289 of reader.py](https://github.com/kinverarity1/lasio/blob/25abedff1ea4d19d41ee88f9bfc9d3f595f0e17a/lasio/reader.py#L289)). 

`sect_lines` remains empty though because lasio doesn't find any lines to add before it hits the next `~`, so `if sect_lines:` (line 290) evaluates to False. Thus the ~P section is never added to the OrderedDict.

However, if you are running Thunderbird on your computer and want to check the unread emails among those stored locally, you can use this (did it a some months ago before noticing there was already an imap module) :

Add the "unread-count" module to Thunderbird : https://addons.mozilla.org/en-US/thunderbird/addon/unread-count/ . It will essentially keep a file with the unread count updated inside, and you can use the following py3status module to find & parse it :

```python
    def getUnreadCount(self, i3s_output_list, i3s_config):
        user = pwd.getpwuid(os.geteuid())[0]
        tbFilePath = "/home/" + user + "/.thunderbird/"
        tbConfig = configparser.ConfigParser()
        profile = "default"
        tbConfig.read(tbFilePath + "profiles.ini")
        profiles = {}
        for section in tbConfig.sections():
            if 'name' in tbConfig[section] and 'path' in tbConfig[section]:
                profiles[tbConfig[section]['name']] = tbConfig[section]['path']
        unreadfile = tbFilePath + profiles[profile] + "/unread-counts"
        with open(unreadfile) as f:
            content = f.readlines()
        unreadCount = 0
        for line in content:
            unreadCount += int(line.split(':')[0])
        response = {
            'cached_until': time() + self.cache_timeout,
            'full_text': str(unreadCount)
        }
        return response
```

One way I can think of to accomplish that is get the FEN of the position, flip all the cases for each piece's letter (p <-> P, k <-> K ... ), flip the castling rights, the enpassant square and the side to move then reparse it.

If you want to do it efficiently you'd probably have to `bitswap` all piece bitboards, color bitboards and occupancy bitboard and then swap the color bitboards. Flip the `turn`, `castling_rights`, `ep_square`, and I'm not exactly sure what the `promoted` member is for. There may be even more depending on the internals but if you don't want to dabble in it, just get the standard FEN, flip that and reparse it like in the first way.

````
from autobahn.twisted.websocket import WebSocketServerProtocol, \
    WebSocketServerFactory
from chatterbot import ChatBot

chatbot = ChatBot(
    'Ron Obvious',
    storage_adapter="chatterbot.adapters.storage.MongoDatabaseAdapter",
    database="chatbot"
)

print chatbot.get_response("Hello, how are you today?")

class MyServerProtocol(WebSocketServerProtocol):

    def onConnect(self, request):
        print("Client connecting: {0}".format(request.peer))

    def onOpen(self):
        print("WebSocket connection open.")

    def onMessage(self, payload, isBinary):
        msg = chatbot.get_response(payload.encode('utf8'))
        self.sendMessage(msg.text.encode('utf8'), isBinary)

    def onClose(self, wasClean, code, reason):
        print("WebSocket connection closed: {0}".format(reason))


if __name__ == '__main__':

    import sys

    from twisted.python import log
    from twisted.internet import reactor

    log.startLogging(sys.stdout)

    factory = WebSocketServerFactory(u"ws://127.0.0.1:9000")
    factory.protocol = MyServerProtocol
    # factory.setProtocolOptions(maxConnections=2)

    # note to self: if using putChild, the child must be bytes...

    reactor.listenTCP(9000, factory)
    reactor.run()

````

`zrange` is really for something different. 

I have added the following options for `heatmap` and `surface`

                    center_scale : float
				Centers the colorscale at a specific value
				Automatically sets the (zmin,zmax) values
			zmin : float
				Defines the minimum range for the z values. 
				This affects the range for the colorscale
			zmax : float
				Defines the maximum range for the z values. 
				This affects the range for the colorscale

So now you can do the following:

```python
cf.datagen.heatmap().iplot(kind='heatmap',colorscale='rdbu',center_scale=0)
```

Which should give you the following:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2511100/38453442-caaca4ac-3a90-11e8-8056-aed20ad14c39.png)

With this you can choose at which value to center the scale, and/or define explicit `zmin` and `zmax` if you want to set a wider range than that defined by the values in the DataFrame.

@bennylope:

In the docs of Haystack:

- https://django-haystack.readthedocs.org/en/latest/searchindex_api.html#stored-indexed-fields

> By default, all fields in Haystack are both indexed (searchable by the engine) and stored (retained by the engine and presented in the results). By using a stored field, you can store commonly used data in such a way that you don’t need to hit the database when processing the search result to get more information.

In the ElasticSearch backend if field is set to indexed=False it is being set to "not_analyzed", which is fine. In terms of ElasticSearch index="no" means that the field is present in the index, but is not searchable.

Quote from ElasticSearch:

- https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/guide/current/mapping-intro.html#_index_2

> The index attribute controls how the string will be indexed. It can contain one of three values:
> 
> - analyzed: First analyze the string and then index it. In other words, index this field as full text.
> - not_analyzed: Index this field, so it is searchable, but index the value exactly as specified. Do not analyze it.
> - no: Don’t index this field at all. This field will not be searchable.

RequireJS can become death by configuration, if you're not careful.

I'd recommend bundling everything in one big JS file, then having a single
entrypoint method that you can use to load page-specific behaviour.

Something like:

{% require_module "main" %}
<script>
    require(["main"], function(main) {
        main.page1();
    });
</script>

大概是 commit 12ac33c (v0.5.3) 引入的Bug。get_stock_data新的实现，每次都会生成一个新的ThreadPool object, 也没有close，导致了内存泄露。考虑到建立thread的开销（比如分配8MB的stack size），每次调用get_stock_data都创建新的thread pool似乎欠妥。简单的将ThreadPool的创建过程移出get_stock_data，并赋予固定大小的pool size，就可以解决这个问题。这样做了get_stock_data是否还是thread safe有待研究。

```
def get_stock_data(self, stock_list, **kwargs):
    pool = ThreadPool(len(stock_list))
    res = pool.map(self.get_stocks_by_range, stock_list)
```

`load_image_file` can load from either a physical file or any python stream. So for example if you have a jpg image stored in a `BytesIO` object, you can pass that right in to `load_image_file`. You can check out the web service example to see how that works.

But if you already have a frame of data from a video, you probably don't need to load anything and can just use the data directly. This library is expecting to work with a 3d numpy array representing the image as 3 channels of 8bit color. If you load a video frame using some other library, you probably already have access to that or could easily convert your data to that format. For example, check out the webcam examples to see how you can use a frame of video from a webcam directly without needing to load anything.

## Does it belong here? (I think yes)
I have discussed it a bit with a colleague, and thought it over: I think this functionality does belong in `gspread-dataframe`: After all, the goal of `gspread-dataframe` is to remove friction from the use of dataframes with Google Sheets (`gspread` specifically).

Your work thus far has removed (all?) the real pain points like batching, offsets, and performance. The remaining problems are minor, and as of now, I can list only two:

- I can't say `get_as_dataframe(...).resample('M', on='date')` without transforming `'date'` myself first.
- Equality fails, as shown above.

If we can make an approximate solution to both problems by just trying out the three different `pd.to_*` functions, I think that is a low-hanging fruit. 


## API

I see three options, each having its pros and cons:

- Being consistent with the API of `pandas.read_csv` (which I think is messy),
- Implementing our own flexible low-level API (which will be verbose),
- Implementing our own brief (but inflexible) high-level API. (Full disclosure: My favourite!)

Here are some suggestions for how we could approach each of the options:

### Consistence with `read_csv`
We could borrow such kwargs as

- **dtype** (a dict where the user specifies the wanted dtypes)
- **parse_dates** (mark columns to parse datetimes from)

However, I get the feeling that the selection of kwargs there is hacked together over a long time; I always found them a bit confusing :-)

### Low-level (high detail)
We could consider a more low-level approach, for instance
- **numeric_cols** (list of cols)
- **datetime_cols** (list of cols)
- **timedelta_cols** (list of cols)

However, IMHO this is already so verbose that the end user might as well do the conversions themself.


### High-level (low detail)
- **infer_dtypes** (bool or list of columns)

where the dataframe, or a subset of its columns, are run through `_assign_column_dtypes`. It could default to False, so that we don't step on anyone's toes by forcefully converting their strings.

What do you think?

Hello,

So, a quick guess is the admin sets the list of contacts using assignation. Doing so would trigger `manager.clear()` on the related manager. If I remember well, Django builds the related manager from the first manager it encounters when reading the model definition. So here, it tries to use `clear` on `FallbackManager`.

That edge case is not tested. You should be able to fix it by making sure the default manager is a regular `TranslationManager`, by specifying it first:

```python
class Event(TranslatableModel):
    objects = TranslationManager()
    fallback = FallbackManager()
```

> If you can propose a way to eliminate the broker, I would be interested in that.

I think you're stuck with it.  If many instances of socketio/ZMQManager can be made (I'm asserting they can), and since the zmq PUB/SUB distribution pattern only works in 1-to-many fashion if the single-point entity _binds_ the socket and the many points _connect_ to the socket, then the ZMQManager instances must always do the _connecting_ on both PUB and SUB.  The "broker" is not a broker but a stub that fills in for the broker required in other platforms like Redis.

> the PUSH/PULL connection is always a one-to-one

Not according to [the zmq documentation](http://zguide.zeromq.org/py:all#Divide-and-Conquer). The figure and text demonstrate that a PUSHer distributes messages (like a card dealer with cards) to whichever PULLers are available to accept them.  If you are attempting to communicate in a manner wherein all clients receive all messages, then this pattern will not work.

BTW, I've tested the PUB/SUB pattern in place of this PUSH/PULL implementation and it works fine.

That just changes the `run.py` script; the problem is with the `.travis.yml` file shell commands.  From the way I understand travis and `sh`, if the command `../../bin/unit` fails, the script keeps executing, it doesn't bubble out the failure to travis.  It needs to be something more like this:

```
    cmake -G "Unix Makefiles" -DASSIMP_ENABLE_BOOST_WORKAROUND=YES -DASSIMP_NO_EXPORT=$TRAVIS_NO_EXPORT -DBUILD_SHARED_LIBS=$SHARED_BUILD ;
    make && sudo make install && sudo ldconfig && (cd test/unit; ../../bin/unit) && (cd ../regression;  chmod 755 run.py; ./run.py) && chmod 755 result_checker.py && ./result_checker.py
```

The giant run-on line is kind of annoying, but I don't think you can have line continuations in the `.travis.yml` file.  I ended up creating a `.travis.sh` script to do my build allowing me access to bash functions and whatever else I wanted without the restrictions imposed by travis for executing shell commands directly from the `.travis.yml` file.

just in case somebody has the same use case, here is what i'm using to backup single databases (bin/backup-one) - including the main Data.fs and blobstorage - see comments below why this is necessary

```
[backup]
recipe = collective.recipe.backup
enable_zipbackup = true

additional_filestorages =
	one ${buildout:directory}/var/filestorage/one.fs ${buildout:directory}/var/blobstorage-one
	two ${buildout:directory}/var/filestorage/two.fs ${buildout:directory}/var/blobstorage-two

[backup-one]
<= backup
# bin/backup -S option not implemented yet - see
# https://github.com/collective/collective.recipe.backup/issues/18

# the following is dangerous, because one database gets exported the same way as
# Data.fs would be if bin/backup was called! 
#blob-storage =  ${buildout:directory}/var/blobstorage-one
#datafs =  ${buildout:directory}/var/filestorage/one.fs
# using additional_filestorages prevents us from this possible failure
# drawback results in the main Data.fs and main blobstorage are beeing backed up multiple times

additional_filestorages = 
  one ${buildout:directory}/var/filestorage/one.fs ${buildout:directory}/var/blobstorage-one
```

It turned out that the index is not actually corrupted, which is good news. What happens is that `git` writes `TREE` extension data into the index, which causes it to write out the given tree *as is* next time a `git commit` is executed. When using `git add`, this extension data is maintained automatically. However, GitPython doesn't do that ... . Usually this is no problem at all, as you are supposed to use `IndexFile.commit(...)` along with `IndexFile.add(...)`.

Thanks to a shortcoming in the GitPython API, the index was automatically written out whenever files have been added, without providing control over whether or not *extension data* will be written along with it.

My fix consists of an additional flag in `IndexFile.add(...)`, which causes extension data not to be written by default, so commits can be safely done via `git commit` or `IndexFile.commit(...)`. 

However, this might introduce new subtle bugs in case someone is relying on extension data to be written. As this can be controlled through the said flag though, a fix is easily done in that case.

### How to apply the fix to your codebase

* Use GitPython *v0.3.7* (due for release this week)
* use `git add` instead of `IndexFile.add(...)`

Unfortunately, there is no other way to make it work with older versions of GitPython, as there is yet another bug in `IndexFile.write(...)` which would cause extension data to written even if that was explicitly disabled. It is fixed in *v0.3.7* .

The problem was that the callback functions receive the resource type and resource name as a pointer to wchar. But the value of the pointer passes the `IS_INTRESOURCE` check that it is interprated as an integer index inastead of a string (see https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/windows/desktop/ms648041%28v=vs.85%29.aspx)

The solutiuon is to claim the input types are LPVOID and wrap the user provided callback in order to convert them into python frieadly types. This results in similar output with the origin win32api library.

The error was not visible before because the `python.exe`does not have resource types defined by name. I have use alternative files that should exist on all windows platforms.

So you're saying you understand the different roles these two files play but you don't see why they can't be captured in the same file? I thought @dstufft's post [explained why clearly](https://caremad.io/posts/2013/07/setup-vs-requirement/):

> ## So Why Does Abstract and Concrete Matter?
> You’ve read this far and maybe you’ve said, ok I know that setup.py is designed for redistributable things and that requirements.txt is designed for non-redistributable things but I already have something that reads a requirements.txt and fills out my install_requires=[...] so why should I care?
> 
> This split between abstract and concrete is an important one. It was what allows the PyPI mirroring infrastructure to work. It is what allows a company to host their own private package index. It is even what enables you to fork a library to fix a bug or add a feature and use your own fork. Because an abstract dependency is a name and an optional version specifier you can install it from PyPI or from Crate.io, or from your own filesystem. You can fork a library, change the code, and as long as it has the right name and version specifier that library will happily go on using it.

A project can only have 1 set of abstract dependencies (`setup.py`/`pyproject.toml`), but different users working with that project can have different sets of concrete dependencies (`requirements.txt`/`Pipfile`) which allow them to fulfill those abstract dependencies from PyPI mirrors, private package indexes, personal forks on GitHub, or somewhere else.

So if project `Car` depends on `Engine` (an abstract dependency), I can choose to install `Car` but grab `Engine` specifically from a fork I made on GitHub (a concrete dependency) that has some performance improvements. Meanwhile, someone else working at a big company that doesn't want to depend on external services to build and deploy their internal Python projects can choose to install both `Car` and `Engine` from their private package index as opposed to PyPI (another concrete dependency).

You can't merge these two types of dependencies together into one file without hampering people's ability to choose where to get their dependencies from.

Does that clarify why `setup.py`/`pyproject.toml` and `requirements.txt`/`Pipfile` need to be different things?

@benjolitz For what it's worth I understand where you are coming from here, and I was mostly trying to reverse engineer the logic for the deprecation myself, having joined this project literally days ago.

In the future, I would agree that it is probably better to use some sort of deprecation cycle that raises warnings if you try and access previously-existing classes like that, since according to [Hyrum's Law](http://www.hyrumslaw.com/) it doesn't matter what the *developer* thinks is part of the public interface, everything accessible *will be used*.

As for your specific concern, I think that `type(parse_version('0.0.0'))` is actually a pretty stable way to get the type of the object returned by `parse_version`, and that idiom is [used in the CPython standard library](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Lib/types.py#L12).

That said, I don't know the function well enough to say that the return type being independent of the input is part of the contract of the function (I can imagine that the contract really just intends to give you objects that have a certain semantic relationship with one another, e.g. they are comparable, without specifying the type). I don't really understand how checking whether the result of `parse_version` would be an instance of `SetuptoolsVersion` would help protect against user hostile input, but I think it's probably a good idea to try to find a way to do it that does not rely on this assumption.

The issue is not with setuptools, but with the way pyinstaller discovers packages. The issue is caused by the fact that setuptools now has to vendor some packages (packaging, six, pyparser), but because it imports them programmatically, pyinstaller fails to detect the need to include those packages.

I imagine there are a couple ways to address the issue.

- setuptools could provide a hook file for pyinstaller to declare which files or modules should be whitelisted. I know the keyring project has had to do something similar for the modules that it programmatically imports.
- application packagers using setuptools could include the dependency packages explicitly.

There is also talk of Setuptools dropping support for installing itself, which would enable it to declare its dependencies and import them naturally, which would also address this issue, but I don't think we can count on that for this issue.

Can someone affected by the pyinstaller issue try creating the hook file?

By default dask.array uses threading, not multiprocessing.  This relies on your computation releasing the GIL.  This is true of most computationally intense numpy functions but may not be true of custom code that isn't mostly using numpy.  This work would be well complimented by going through computationally intense scikit-image cython code and releasing the GIL at key locations.

You can use other schedulers if you choose but the costs of data transfer in multiprocessing are high, possibly higher than you'll gain.  There is a multiprocessing scheduler and a nascent distributed memory scheduler.  

You can set a global default on the scheduler as follows:

```python
import dask
from dask.multiprocessing import get as mpget
dask.set_options(get=mpget)
```

Generally one does this without globals with a keyword argument to the compute method

```python
x = da.random.normal(10, 1, size=(100000, 100000), chunks=(1000, 1000))
x.mean().compute(get=mpget)
```

We should probably pass through a get `kwarg` down to compute in this PR.

One thought I had was about performance.  Implementing `__eq__` is going to have a cost over using the default identity-based implementation. Simply overriding it to do identity comparison makes it more than twice as slow:
```python
In [1]: class WithEq(object):
   ...:     def __eq__(self, other):
   ...:         return other is self
   ...:

In [2]: class NoEq(object): pass

In [3]: noeq = NoEq()

In [4]: witheq = WithEq()

In [5]: %timeit noeq == noeq
The slowest run took 19.87 times longer than the fastest. ...
10000000 loops, best of 3: 108 ns per loop

In [6]: %timeit witheq == witheq
The slowest run took 10.28 times longer than the fastest. ...
1000000 loops, best of 3: 278 ns per loop
```

We'll also need to override `__hash__` so that it's consistent with the `__eq__` definition and that seems to take an even bigger hit:
```python
In [9]: class WithHash(object):
   ...:     def __hash__(self):
   ...:         return 1
   ...:

In [10]: class NoHash(object): pass

In [11]: withhash = WithHash()

In [12]: nohash = NoHash()

In [13]: %timeit hash(withhash)
The slowest run took 13.90 times longer than the fastest. ...
1000000 loops, best of 3: 223 ns per loop

In [14]: %timeit hash(nohash)
The slowest run took 25.18 times longer than the fastest. ...
10000000 loops, best of 3: 85.2 ns per loop
```

Hopefully this isn't actually a problem...

OK I see, it's a good opportunity for you to learn a bit more about git/GitHub and the shell.
Basically if you used the online editor, what it does is it creates a fork. So you can simply clone it:
```sh
git clone git@github.com:kuzeyron/python-for-android.git
```
And then work on it, the workflow should be something like:
```sh
cd python-for-android/
git reset --soft HEAD~1
vim pythonforandroid/recipes/feedparser/__init__.py
tox
git add pythonforandroid/recipes/feedparser/__init__.py
git commit --amend
```
Basically the `git reset --soft HEAD~1` will squash your two commits and create only one. Then you edit with your favorite editor (vim obviously). Then you run the `tox` test to make sure you didn't introduce any regression. Then you cheat the commit log with `git add && git commit --amend`.
Feel free to Google the commands you didn't understand to find out more.

Thanks so much for the PR @faridasabry2 :smile: I can see now how `client` and `keen` are confusing in the README. You can assign an instance of `KeenClient` to `client` (or to any other variable name) so you can have multiple Keen projects configured in the same python file. If we assigned the instance of `KeenClient` to be `keen` on [line 39](https://github.com/keenlabs/KeenClient-Python/pull/57/files#diff-04c6e90faac2675aa89e2176d2eec7d8L39), we'd be reassigning `keen` on [line 28](https://github.com/keenlabs/KeenClient-Python/pull/57/files#diff-04c6e90faac2675aa89e2176d2eec7d8L28), which is the originally imported Keen module. Consequentially, we wouldn't be able to access the Keen library (which also has the default project configuration). For example, let's say we have a project called "Hacks at Smith" that uses `keen` to track PRs submitted, and then we start working on another project called "#CatShirtThursday Recruits". We'd want to give our #CatShirtThursday project its own instance of `KeenClient` (e.g. `client`) so we can continue to collect and analyze data from the "Hacks at Smith" project.

```python
    import keen
    from keen.client import KeenClient

    keen.project_id = "<Project ID for Hacks at Smith>"
    keen.write_key = "yyyy"
    keen.read_key = "zzzz"
    keen.master_key = "abcd"

    client = KeenClient(
        project_id="<Project ID for #CatShirtThursday Recruits>",
        write_key="yyyy",
        read_key="zzzz",
        master_key="abcd"
    )

    keen.count("PRs") # returns the number of PRs submitted at the hackathon we're tracking 
    client.count("catshirt recruits") # returns the number of new recruits for #CatShirtThursday 
```

The rest of the documentation uses `keen` to reflect the idea of using Keen IO for a single project, and uses the instance of `KeenClient` defined on [line 28](https://github.com/keenlabs/KeenClient-Python/pull/57/files#diff-04c6e90faac2675aa89e2176d2eec7d8L28). Does that make sense?

If you want to submit a PR that clarifies `keen` and `client`, I'd love to include it in the README. If not, I'll try to update the PR sometime soon.

Also, Hack@Smith sounds pretty sweet! Send me an email at stephanie(at)keen(dot)io -- I'd love to connect and send you thanks for contributing to the Keen Python SDK.

If you get a cached distribution (different from the index) then it will also download a cache file which is a tar.gz of all of the package.xml's which will be even slower. However, there really isn't an alternative for some tools, like `rosinstall_generator`. We implement local caching with this library, but it would be possible, the API is pretty modular.

```
A--------B
\----C---/
```

so you could define a "fast" transform from A to B, but could also go from A to some intermediate C and then from C to B?  Is seems that the dictionary structure allows for that, but the code, which always picks the first item in the "to frame" dictionary, wouldn't support it.

These types are not supported natively in the new Datastore API and instead we rely on clients to implement these as necessary on a per-application basis. The rational here is that the Datastore API should be as simple as possible and push application logic up the stack. For example with the `PostalAddress` type it may make sense for the application to validate/normalize the address (for example by using the Google Maps API), then storing it as a String in Datastore. Importantly, Datastore doesn't/shouldn't know what that string represents.

For these types which were stored using the App Engine Datastore libraries, they are accessible as more generic types. For example, storing a User property in App Engine would look like an `entity_value` property in the Cloud Datastore API with `email`, `auth_domain`, and `user_id` properties.

The `PostalAddress` type is stored as a `string_value` (which is how it is actually stored in Datastore).

@bittner 
A working proof-of-concept (POC) helps the discussion what is needed. From my understanding, it is possible by using the "environment.py" file and the existing hooks. If something is missing, it will also become clearer what is missing and where. Then, a second design can be made that implements the same functionality by using  plugins (and allows comparison to the first solution). We can discuss/finetune it until we have an agreeable solution (and identify the extension-points that are needed and for which a plugin can register itself).

**Regarding plugins injected automatically via installed packages:**
This does not work from my experience (and bites you in the back). For example, ``behave-django`` provides such a plugin (or several) and ``behave-django`` is installed. Therefore, the ``behave-django`` plugin is always and automatically used during each behave run. But now you have a case, where the behave tests do not use any django related functionality. We do no want that this plugin is automatically used when we are in this project/directory context.

Therefore, plugins installed with packages are OK (also the normal entry-point concept), but you need a configuration layer that allows the user to stay in control and enable only the plugins that are needed (and desired) in a specific test scope. As I have already said, the behave's userdata functionality could be used to provide transport-vehicle for such a configuration layer (from the command-line or configuration-file to the "environment.py" file). The acting logic that applies the configuration (for example to ``behave-django`` and/or ``django``) is still needed.

Any suggestions how to improve the discussion/development of such a concept are welcome (own playground project on Github to evolve the solution, Hangout meetings, Slack group, etc.).

Ah okay, well I've figured it out! Just had to reshape the data a little bit before giving it to `imageio`. Here's a little snippet that saves an animation:

```python
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import imageio
from numpy import sin, pi

time = np.linspace(0, 10, 100)
with imageio.get_writer('test.gif', mode='I', fps=24) as writer:
	print('Working...')
	for t in time:

		x = np.linspace(-2*pi,2*pi, 100)
		plt.plot(x, (x/(2*pi))*sin(t+x))
		plt.grid()
		plt.xlim(-2*pi, 2*pi), plt.ylim(-1.2, 1.2)
		fig = plt.gcf()
		fig.canvas.draw()
		data = fig.canvas.tostring_rgb()
		rows, cols = fig.canvas.get_width_height()

		imarray = np.fromstring(data, dtype=np.uint8).reshape(cols, rows, 3)
		writer.append_data(imarray)
		plt.close()
print('Done.')
```

You have to add a default behavior for Google Drive-files which you do not want to download. My suggestion would be to just skip all files with a Google Drive mimetype which you are not explicitely handling (such as folders). 

Thus, the code would look like this:
```
for file1 in file_list:
    download_mimetype = None
    current_mimetype = file1['mimeType']

    #  Check if file's mime type is Google Drive-file type.
    if 'application/vnd.google-apps' in current_mimetype:
        
        if file1['mimeType'] in mimetypes:  # Check if mime type is handled.
            download_mimetype = mimetypes[file1['mimeType']]
        else:  # Skip file if mime type is not handled.
            continue

    file1.GetContentFile(file1['title'], mimetype=download_mimetype)
```

Note, however, that Google Drive has no naming restrictions, so downloading all files into one folder can easily result in name collisions. Such collisions would lead to files with the same name being overwritten.

Hey Randy, I think I found the problem. I had reworked the method for performance problems with large data sets. Trying to create a large dataframe from streamed data doesn't scale. So I now create an empty data frame and build out and adjust the data types on a temp dataframe, per chunk of streamed data, and append that temp df to the one I'll inevitably return. The append method doesn't behave consistently. Sometimes the final df gets the dtypes of the temp one I append, while other times it doesn't. I've tweaked my code to account for this - don't create the final (empty) df up front. The first time I would append, just set it to be that temp one (instead of appending) so it for sure has the right data types; not maybe the right and maybe the wrong.

@obmarg it is applicable to deis.  Deis apps all have an owner and optionally a list of privileged collaborators.  If you are not on that list for a given app, you can not deploy, view or edit configuration, releases, etc., etc.

Real life scenario: in a large org, a central devops team maintains a large deis cluster used for _development_ by multiple depts. / lines of business.  One area has created `product-a-dev.example.com`.  Another dept. has no privileges with respect to that application, yet if they create an application of their own with the custom domain `*.example.com` they can completely hijack the routing and have traffic intended for the first app flowing to their own.

I appreciate that this is a bug for you.  This is _extremely_ unusual, except in cases where the cluster is heavily overburdened.  The `delete` API call [checks for exceptions](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/master/curator/api/delete.py#L16-L24), and if there are none, it returns True.  This is why it's considered a successful operation.  Elasticsearch itself is capable of deleting hundreds of indices simultaneously when not under egregious loads.  The test suite itself does this with many indices simultaneously.  If your cluster is failing to do this, you should look at the Elasticsearch logs to see what's happening.  Even the time elapsed in the log you provided suggests it sent all of the deletes in a 2 second window.

What happens if you run this with the `--debug` flag?  If you're trying to delete older than 7 days, why are you waiting until there are 5 indices of every kind, rather than running daily to prune the number of indices more regularly?  How many nodes in your cluster?  How many shards per node?  The count of indices you have is concerning and could be a contributing factor.

here is difference how aiohttp handles timeout.

1.3 - timeout get applied only to connection + send request, after that there is no timeout on operation
2.0 - timeout get applied to all operations related to request, connection, send request, get response.

in your test you create all connections at the same time, so aiohttp tries to connect and start timeout at the same time. but remote service can not serve you requests fast enough, so at some point you start to see timeout errors. I start to see timeouts around 450 requests, so aiohttp able to complete about 450 request within first 4 seconds. and that is the reason why you can not reproduce this with local server, it just able to serve all your requests within 4 seconds.

also `asyncio.gather` does not protect you from exceptions, (shield() does)
lets say you try to gather 100 requests (coroutines), but if one of those raises exception (TimeoutError in your case) it cancels `asyncio.gather()` operation. for crawler case you can not really just send request and wait response, you have to handle exceptions from requests, bad responses, etc and return result.
generally, you have to "shield" request if you want to use it with `gather()`

@AngusG Nevertheless, I don't see why a simple dense net shouldn't work the way it is right now. For me, this works:

```python3
import foolbox
import tensorflow as tf
import numpy as np

inputs = tf.placeholder(tf.float32, shape=(None, 784))
logits = tf.layers.dense(inputs, 10)

init_op = tf.global_variables_initializer()

np.random.seed(2)
W = np.random.rand(784, 10).astype(np.float32)

assign_op = tf.assign(tf.global_variables()[0], W)

np.random.seed(22)
example_input = np.random.rand(784)

with foolbox.models.TensorFlowModel(inputs, logits, (0, 1)) as model:
    attack = foolbox.attacks.FGSM(model)

    model.session.run(init_op)
    model.session.run(assign_op)  # replace with restorer

    example_label = np.argmax(model.predictions(example_input))
    print(example_label)

    adversarial = attack(example_input, example_label, unpack=False)
    print(np.argmax(model.predictions(adversarial.image)))

    print(adversarial.distance)
```

Regarding that set of methods:
```
 Organization.subscriptions
 Organization.upload_manifest
 Organization.delete_manifest
 Organization.refresh_manifest
```
It was mentioned in the previous comments that it will be good to drop `Organization.upload_manifest` method in favor of `Subscription.upload`. And, that idea seems really great and must be implemented in that way if we have proper API path like this:
`POST /katello/api/subscriptions/upload` for all methods, but that is not true at that moment:

Organization.subscriptions:
`GET /katello/api/subscriptions/:id`
Organization.upload_manifest:
`POST /katello/api/subscriptions/upload`
Organization.delete_manifest:
`POST /katello/api/organizations/:organization_id/subscriptions/delete_manifest` (*Only this*)
Organization.refresh_manifest:
`PUT /katello/api/organizations/:organization_id/subscriptions/refresh_manifest` (*Only this*)

As we do not have proper implementation for all paths for mentioned functionality from `Subscription` entity point of view, we should proceed with old approach (left all these methods in `Organization` entity). Otherwise, we will have things like that:
```
subscription.upload_manifest()
org.delete_manifest()
```
which is obviously not good idea, especially from tool user perspective

So here's the thing. When I said overriding the event loop would be the way to go, I actually tested it out first using the following code:

```
import asyncio

import pytest


class CustomSelectorLoop(asyncio.SelectorEventLoop):
    """A subclass with no overrides, just to test for presence."""
    pass


@pytest.fixture()
def event_loop(request):
    """Create an instance of the default event loop for each test case."""
    policy = asyncio.get_event_loop_policy()

    policy.get_event_loop().close()

    loop = CustomSelectorLoop()
    policy.set_event_loop(loop)

    request.addfinalizer(loop.close)
    return loop
```
as you can see, the `event_loop` fixture is a little more complex to write. Your fix might solve the problem for you, but chances are third-party libraries might want to get the current event loop using asyncio anyway, and then they'll get another loop.

What I propose we do instead is move the logic of setting the produced event loop outside of the `event_loop` fixture and into pytest-asyncio itself.

```
@pytest.mark.tryfirst
def pytest_pyfunc_call(pyfuncitem):
    """
    Run asyncio marked test functions in an event loop instead of a normal
    function call.
    """
    for marker_name, fixture_name in _markers_2_fixtures.items():
        if marker_name in pyfuncitem.keywords:
            event_loop = pyfuncitem.funcargs[fixture_name]

            policy = asyncio.get_event_loop_policy()
            policy.get_event_loop().close()
            policy.set_event_loop(event_loop)

            funcargs = pyfuncitem.funcargs
            testargs = {arg: funcargs[arg]
                        for arg in pyfuncitem._fixtureinfo.argnames}
            event_loop.run_until_complete(
                asyncio.async(pyfuncitem.obj(**testargs)))

            event_loop.close()
            return True
```

```
@pytest.fixture
def event_loop(request):
    """Create an instance of the default event loop for each test case."""
    policy = asyncio.get_event_loop_policy()
    return policy.new_event_loop()
```

@cornelinux That is not what `tzinfos` is supposed to do.

Consider that if you just want to attach a time zone to the date, you can just do this:

```python
dt = parse(dt_str).replace(tzinfo=tzlocal())
```

What `tzinfos` is supposed to do is take a dictionary mapping time zone strings to time zones (e.g. `tzinfos={'EST': tzoffset(timedelta=hours-5'), 'CST': tzoffset(timedelta(hours=-6))}`) or a callable that takes time zone strings and generates `tzinfo` objects. It was just a random mistake that `tzlocal` was ignoring erroneous arguments thus causing this confusion for you.

One other thing to note is that (at least in Python 3, I don't know about Python 2) `datetime.astimezone()` considers naive dates to be in local time, so if you want to assume that naive times are in local time, you can just convert the dates directly to any time zone you want, even if they have an attached time zone (e.g. `parse(dt_str).astimezone(tz.tzutc())` will just give you the time in UTC assuming naive times are local).

On second thought, I was wrong about symmetry and `dump(load(stuff)) == struff`.

As is, it makes no sense, what I meant was more like

```python
MyObject(**load(dump(input_obj)) == input_obj
```

and I don't think there's a way to achieve this in all cases.

`dump` expects an object. `None` is an object like the others and if we create an exception for `None` (raise `ValidationError` rather than return `{}`), why not an exception for `int`s, `float`s, etc? `dump` must return an empty `dict` if the attributes can't be found, regardless of the type of the input object. `load` returns an empty `dict` if provided an empty `dict`.

An object can be instantiated from the load of the dump of its own data only if the schema loads all necessary data and it the object class knows how to create it with this data.

Hm, two wheels would probably not work to support the same versions as today... pySerial currently runs on Python >=3.2. With a feature only working on Python 3.4 onward, it would require separate wheels for every version, as I understand it, the specification only allows all of Python (universal) the all of 2.x respectively 3.x or single versions, but not ranges of versions (even when it did, it would still require 3 packages).

The other option would be two make a second package. That way, pySerial could stay "universal" and the Python 3.4 (or even 3.5) features would be in a separate package, e.g. `pyserial-asyncio`. It would also make sense regarding "API stability" and supported OS. I tend to this solution.

This is caused by the way Mythril resolves function names.

There's a `signatures.json` file in the user's home directory that contains mappings of known function hashes to function names. If the function hash is not in the file, Mythril uses the generic `_function_0x...` identifiers. 

Every time you analyze a Solidity file, the hashes from that file are added to `signatures.json`.

What makes things more complicated is that there's a [default signatures file](https://github.com/ConsenSys/mythril/blob/master/signatures.json), but I think this is currently not installed automatically. So if you install Mythril you start with an empty one.

Also, in your example, I would actually expect it to work the first time (the function names should be available after the compilation step).

Thanks for double checking. 

You can see the algorithm fail for very small PW in the lowest sections of the contour plots in my initial comment. This is because PW is parameterized in powers of ``ln(w)``, and ``ln(w)`` goes to ``-inf`` as ``w`` goes to 0. The AOD parameterization only contains powers of AOD, so that has good lower limit behavior. The ``ln(p/p0)`` pressure parameterization should work for any reasonable user input. I think that it's reasonable for users to expect the algorithm to work for ``AOD --> 0`` and ``PW --> 0`` (clean, dry atmosphere), but not ``p --> 0`` (high flying solar drones?).

I'm less worried about the upper limits since I don't think that they will be reached using real data and because users should naturally be skeptical of the upper limits.

Two options for addressing this *after* removing pluralizing from `ObjectMapper.convert_dt_name`

1)  Extend `ObjectMapper` to override `convert_dt_name` to pluralize, and register this new subclass as the mapper for those classes that relied on this behavior.

@aaltat and I did two changes today to ease extending:

- There's now `element_finder` attribute in the library itself that can be overridden by extending classes. Notice that it's API isn't exactly the same as in earlier versions, though.
- `PythonLibCore` that the library uses internally got new `add_library_components` method that can be used to register new library components after making calling `__init__` of the parent class.

We hope that these changes make it easier to extend the library in general. Hopefully also fixing the code that gets broken due to the architecture changes isn't too complicated. Old extending approaches have used private interfaces and being fully compatible with them is not possible.

Architecture changes ought to be now done except that we still need to fix registering custom locator strategies. After that we are very close to alpha 1 release.

The situation from my perspective is this:

1  We want an isolated transfer mechanism which allows us to determine where to transfer the file to with ideally no copies.
2  This should effectively be the `copyTo` method which we can chose to expose at an implementation level which handles user requests
3  The get method should call the transfer with the `localDir` as an argument
4  We want to stop requiring that the `get` method auto-determines if the object is owned by a job and transferring the file to the jobs folders. --- To me this is this is _at least_ another PR of work as it's not clear at all where/why this behaviour was expected. There should be direct calls to request a file be sent somewhere when we want it send somewhere not `localDir` but as I've stated this is moving beyond the scope of this work to get the `LocalFile` to behave in the same way.

I definitely think it would be good to document the branch and release situation so that users can make an informed decision on which version to use or what will be best for their use case.

A couple more suggestions:

Keep main releases (from master branch) consistent in terms of naming convention with logical semantic versioning. For example, calling one release "v1.9.7" and the next release "v1.10.0 for the ZR300" makes it sound like v1.10.0 is only for the ZR300.

If there are intermediate releases based on the development branch (or another branch) make sure this is clearly highlighted in the name and decide whether semantic versioning should apply to that release.

I think by default users will always grab the latest release based on semantic version so these should always be community releases from master (or pre-release). Anything released specific to a customer should be easily distinguishable from mainline releases if they are not suitable for general use.

    def viewer_setup(self):
        self.viewer.cam.trackbodyid = 0         # 0: camera fixed, 1: camera tracking the moving object

        self.viewer.cam.lookat[1] += 0.5

        self.viewer.cam.elevation = 90           # camera rotation around the axis in the plane going through the frame origin (if 0 you just see a line)

@20chase it looks like `patchelf` is unable to find `/usr/lib/nvidia-384/libOpenGL.so.0`. My advice is to install `mlocate` if you haven't already and run the following:

```
sudo updatedb
locate libOpenGL.so
```

This will search your computer for all files that contain the name `libOpenGL.so`. You may need to change [this line](https://github.com/openai/mujoco-py/blob/master/mujoco_py/builder.py#L312) to match the path that you find.

@ronshaar, a clarification about being able to assign variables using exec in Python 3:

You actually cannot assign a “local” variable using exec.

>>> def f():
...     a = 1
...     exec("a = 3")
...     print(a)
... 
>>> f()
1
>>> 

So, in that case, declaring the local variable first just means that you end up using that first declared value.  You can still set global variables with exec, though:

>>> a = 1
>>> exec("a = 3")
>>> a
3

Exec also still works for setting things, like wx.SetBackgroundColour, but it’s definitely something to be cautious of.  

So, what was happening in the code fix that you did in c35921c is that the variable was being initialized to ‘’ but couldn’t be re-set by exec.  

Fortunately, exec wasn’t really necessary in this function anyway.  Here’s my update:  b7c514d.  I’ve tested it with a modified version of the example file in data_files/orientation_magic (modified to include some additional headers and make sure we are hitting everything), and it seems to work correctly.  Let me know if you find that this doesn’t work the way you expect!

> will native resize operation on converted array be supported on Python and/or C++ side?

This isn't even supported in numpy itself when you have have referencing arrays:

```python
>>> z = np.array([[1,2],[3,4]])
>>> y = z.transpose()
>>> z.resize((4,4))
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
ValueError: cannot resize an array that references or is referenced
by another array in this way.  Use the resize function
>>> y.resize((4,4))
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
ValueError: cannot resize this array: it does not own its data
```
so I can't see any way it could be supported here.

You probably *can* do it in Eigen, but I'm pretty sure that when doing so you immediately invalidate any previous references, which is going to include numpy -> eigen references.  So you really don't want to do that.

To expand on this issue, some versions of the VISA library automatically send `*CLS;` on receiving a viClear. This sequence seems to crash older versions of the decaDac. See: http://digital.ni.com/public.nsf/allkb/4A8D61E759174E7F86256E20005A8ABC

A couple of options we could choose between:
 - Don't viClear when opening an instrument. This is probably the simplest option, my preferred.
 - If we are worried about junk in the IO buffers, use viFlush instead:
```py3
self.visa_handle.flush(visa.constants.VI_READ_BUF_DISCARD | visa.constants.VI_WRITE_BUF_DISCARD)
```
 - Set the VISA flags given in the linked document such that we don't assume that VISA instruments support SCPI commands.

As for number 1), unfortunately this (server resources) can't be implemented in queue as you have written it. However I do *believe* that there are __equivalent__ formulations that can be simulated.

One that immediately springs to mind is described below, however we must drop the assumption that customers are willing to wait up to 30 minutes for a bike. Assuming they baulk as soon as there are no bicycles:

- Think of the bicycles as customers.
- Bicycles batch arrive (100 at a time, every 60 minutes, using the method described above ^^^). They join a queue to be rented.
- This queue is single server, and the service distribution is the same as the original customer's arrival distribution.

This is equivalent as the amount of time a bicycle is spent at the node is identical to the amount of time a bicycle is spend waiting for a customer in the original scenario. In this case we do not care about the customers as they do not wait, they simply arrive and take a bike, thus the only information we require is the inter-arrival time, which is now modelled as a service time.

Does that make sense?
Now this model would need to be adapted to satisfy all details of the original model (e.g. customers willing to wait up to 30 minutes for a bike). But this is quite advanced customer behaviour that isn't supported by Ciw unfortunately.

Reading through the comments above, it looks like recording a stream without Streamlink doing the actual file-writing [is trivial](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/issues/1175#issuecomment-322942132), even on Windows.

**"Write programs that do one thing and do it well."**
- Leave the file-writing to others and concentrate on your core functionality.
- Recording may be a nice feature, well, allow me to call it a "gimmick" (no offense intended) for the sake of my argument, but it is out of scope for this project. The only reason it's there is because it's Livestreamer-heritage. I know this is hard to swallow for the people who actually use it and got used to it, but think of it this way: if Streamlink did not have a recording option today and someone would request this feature, would you implement it or would you show him/her how trivial it is to pipe Streamlink's output into a program that can do just that?

Do not reinvent the wheel / reimplement features that are readily available by simply piping output from program A to program B.

Hi,

just replace
`q.addRequestedResult(RequestArticlesUriList())`
with 
`q.setRequestedResult(RequestArticlesUriList())`

The reason for the error is that constructor of `QueryArticles` by default already sets article list as a return type. In your case, you want return data type to be article URI list, which is why you need to override the default choice. Multiple `addRequesterResult()`  calls for the same query are allowed only for the paying customers.

@jasongrout It appears by default `float('inf')` is used if, for example max is not provided – this is in the traitlets package [[1](https://github.com/ipython/traitlets/blob/master/traitlets/traitlets.py#L2037-L2038)]. 

Also, `inf` serializes to `Infinity` when using the standard library `json`.

```python
import json
json.dumps({'x': float('inf')})
# '{"x": Infinity}'
```

And Javascript also supports `Infinity`. Using `None` may be more challenging.
